summary: This paper proposes a rank-statistic approach to approximating f-divergences that sidesteps the usual need for explicit density-ratio estimation. Using the probability integral transform, the discrepancy between two univariate distributions mu and nu is encoded through the distribution of ranks: for a resolution parameter K, one forms a rank histogram on {0,...,K} and measures its deviation from uniformity via a discrete f-divergence, yielding a rank-statistic divergence estimator. The authors prove that this estimator is monotone in K, always lower-bounds the true f-divergence, and converges to it as K goes to infinity under mild regularity of the quantile-domain density ratio, with quantitative rates. To move beyond one dimension, they introduce a sliced rank-statistic f-divergence by averaging the univariate construction over random projections, and they provide convergence guarantees for the sliced limit. The paper further establishes finite-sample deviation bounds and asymptotic normality for the estimator, and validates the approach empirically by benchmarking against neural estimators and by using it as an optimization-free objective for generative transport dynamics, including on image data such as CelebA.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The central idea is genuinely appealing. Density-ratio estimation is the standard obstacle to making f-divergences practical, and replacing it with a rank-based construction that operates directly on the distribution of ranks is a fresh and well-motivated angle. The synthesis of the probability integral transform, rank histograms, and sliced discrepancies is elegant, and extending rank-based lower bounds to general differentiable f-generators is a nice theoretical contribution. On soundness, the one-dimensional theory is rigorous and reasonably complete: the estimator is shown to be a monotone lower bound to the true divergence, convergence rates as K grows are established under mild regularity of the quantile-domain density ratio, and the paper additionally provides finite-sample deviation bounds and asymptotic normality. The sliced extension to high dimensions is a natural and sensible way to retain the univariate machinery, and the overall development is coherent, although I did not verify every appendix proof line by line. On significance, estimating f-divergences in high dimensions without an intermediate density-ratio step targets a real bottleneck, and the optimization-free character of the estimator is attractive as a stable surrogate; the experiments indicate it is competitive with neural estimators and usable in non-trivial tasks.

The main reservations concern presentation, empirical validation, and the precise framing of the method's utility. Presentation is the weakest aspect. There is substantial notational forward-referencing -- for instance, empirical measures and sample variables are introduced and used in the empirical-estimation discussion well before they are formally defined in Section 2.2 -- and several symbols (e.g., b_{n,K}, ran, F-hat for the sliced measures, and H_r-Holder continuity in the appendix) appear without definition. There is also notation shadowing between the sliced and non-sliced estimators that would benefit from an explicit note, Example 2.2 (total variation) is not fully clear, and there are typographical errors in the proofs (notably around Lemma B.3 and Theorem 2.5). Material currently in the appendix (e.g., Section C.6) appears important enough for a practical reading of the method to warrant promotion to the main text. These issues do not appear to affect correctness, but they meaningfully hinder readability.

On the empirical side, the low-dimensional experiments are useful and probably necessary to validate the approximation, but they do not yet fully establish that the estimator captures the distinct behavior of different f-divergences in realistic settings. For example, in the precision-recall style results one would expect a KL-based model to achieve better recall than a Jensen-based one, and this is not clearly borne out (Table 4). Controlled stress tests -- duplicated samples, dropped modes, or injected out-of-distribution examples -- would more convincingly separate the divergences and strengthen the case, and the value of the estimator for evaluation on modern, genuinely high-dimensional generative models is asserted more than demonstrated. I am also not fully convinced by the transport-based generation procedure as a general training objective: while it can produce samples, it is not obviously a tractable, broadly useful learning objective in the way f-GAN is, and the abstract and experiments would be more accurate if they positioned the method primarily as an evaluation and sampling tool. Two further practical gaps are the absence of concrete guidance for selecting the resolution K and the number of projections L for a new target (currently left as an open problem), and the lack of a wall-clock and memory comparison against neural variational estimators in the high-dimensional experiments.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide concrete, actionable guidance -- a rule of thumb or an adaptive schedule -- for choosing the resolution K and the number of projections L when applying the method to a new target distribution?
2. How does the rank-statistic estimator compare to standard neural variational estimators in terms of wall-clock time and memory footprint in the high-dimensional generative experiments (e.g., CelebA)?
3. Could you add controlled experiments (duplicated samples, dropped modes, out-of-distribution injection) that isolate the behavior of different f-divergences? In particular, in Table 4, why does the KL-minimizing model not achieve the better recall one might expect relative to the Jensen-based one?
4. How does the approach relate to precision-recall curves interpreted as f-divergences and to density-ratio-free estimators in that line of work (e.g., Sajjadi et al.; Verine et al.; Siry et al.; Simon et al.), and how would it behave when estimating divergences in pretrained feature spaces such as Inception, DINOv2, or VGG?
5. In the particle-transport construction of Section 4.4, is F-hat intended to be the empirical CDF R-hat, and can the construction of its inverse from samples be made explicit? Relatedly, is there a symmetric counterpart to Theorem 3.3 when the roles of the empirical and reference measures are swapped?
limitations: The choice of the resolution K and the number of projections L is acknowledged as an open problem, but the practical scope of the method deserves more explicit discussion: in particular, whether it is best positioned as a training objective or as an evaluation/sampling tool, and its computational cost and reliability in genuinely high-dimensional settings. Making these boundaries explicit would give a more accurate picture of where the method is expected to work well.
